topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion in the otherwise calm rhythm of my afternoon. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years—a grainy photo taken at some forgotten summer camp, the subject sporting a smirk that instantly tightened something cold in my stomach. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of scraped knees and whispered cruelties in the third grade. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, trembling slightly. Why now? After all this time, after everything that happened, this digital outreach felt like a deliberate, strange ripple in still water. I leaned back in my chair, the plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the screen, my gaze darting between the familiar, irritating face and the sudden, unwelcome awareness of the past.

[story 2]

I almost deleted the request. My thumb hovered over the swipe gesture, a physical resistance fighting the urge to simply erase the intrusion. The message was sparse: "Hey. Long time no talk." My jaw felt suddenly tight, the muscles in my neck corded. I reread the text three times, searching for some hidden trap, some veiled threat masked as casual greeting. I glanced around my small apartment, the afternoon sun slanting through the blinds, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air. A strange, hollow feeling settled over me, a sense that the established boundaries of my life had just been subtly, carelessly redrawn. I took a slow, deep breath, the air tasting thin, and finally tapped 'Accept.'

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm tea. The ceramic mug felt suddenly heavy in my hands, the warmth doing nothing to soothe the prickling sensation running down my spine. It was him. The face that used to elicit a sharp retort or a quick retreat from a crowded hallway. My eyes scanned the profile—the slightly altered haircut, the confident set of his shoulders in the picture. A slow, almost imperceptible shake ran through my head. I set the mug down with a soft clink. I didn't want to engage, yet I couldn't look away. It was like encountering a ghost wearing a familiar disguise, something deeply unsettling about the sudden reappearance of that particular shadow. I just sat there, perfectly still, waiting for the next move.
